title: EU Green Fuel Mandates Reshape Summer Tourism Economics - The Traveler
description:
Specialized fuel price assessments describe strong structural demand, restricted supplies and a noticeable premium for sustainable aviation fuel blends compared with standard jet fuel.
Tourism economists note that fuel costs typically account for around a quarter or more of airline operating expenses.
Overlaying this climate-driven redistribution with rising access costs from sustainable aviation fuel mandates creates a complex picture for local tourism economies.
For small island destinations within the EU that depend almost entirely on air links, sustainable aviation fuel mandates pose a different challenge.
Estimates circulated by these groups suggest that sustainable aviation fuel may cover well under 1 percent of global jet fuel demand in 2025.
text:
Europe’s first binding sustainable aviation fuel mandates are starting to reshape the price and pattern of summer travel, as airlines, destinations and travelers adjust to a new cost base for flying in and around the European Union.

A New Cost Floor for Peak-Season Flights

The European Union’s ReFuelEU Aviation regulation, which began to apply to fuel suppliers in January 2025, requires that at least 2 percent of jet fuel made available at EU airports be sustainable aviation fuel, with the share rising to 6 percent in 2030 and eventually 70 percent by mid-century. Publicly available policy summaries describe the requirement as a volume-based mandate on suppliers, tied to all flights departing EU airports regardless of destination.

Because sustainable aviation fuel currently costs significantly more than conventional kerosene, the obligation is creating a higher structural cost base for airlines operating in and out of the bloc. Industry analyses cited by aviation bodies indicate that the price premium attached to sustainable aviation fuel added several billion dollars to global airline fuel bills in 2025, even at relatively low blending levels.

As the 2026 summer season approaches, market outlooks point to a tight balance between sustainable aviation fuel supply and fast-rising demand from airlines trying to comply with the mandate. Specialized fuel price assessments describe strong structural demand, restricted supplies and a noticeable premium for sustainable aviation fuel blends compared with standard jet fuel. That premium is beginning to be reflected in fare structures, particularly on fuel-intensive long-haul routes linking Europe with North America and Asia.

Tourism economists note that fuel costs typically account for around a quarter or more of airline operating expenses. In that context, an additional sustainable aviation fuel premium, on top of recent volatility in global oil markets, is reinforcing the upward pressure on high-season airfares that many travelers already experienced in the summers of 2024 and 2025.

Airlines Rework Networks and Pricing Strategies

Airlines serving Europe are responding to the new regulatory environment with a mix of fare adjustments, network tweaks and procurement strategies. Public statements and investor presentations from several European carriers in late 2025 and early 2026 point to expectations of higher average unit costs tied directly to sustainable aviation fuel obligations, emissions trading costs and planned energy taxation of fossil jet fuel within Europe.

Analysts tracking airline schedules for the upcoming summer report that some carriers are consolidating marginal leisure routes, concentrating capacity on trunk holiday markets where higher fares can be absorbed more easily. Intra-European point-to-point services to secondary coastal airports appear more exposed to rationalization, while major Mediterranean hubs are expected to retain or even grow capacity thanks to strong demand and better economies of scale.

Pricing is also evolving. Revenue-management data cited in travel industry coverage indicate that carriers are increasingly using dynamic surcharges and branded fare bundles to pass through environmental and fuel-related costs. While many airlines avoid labelling any specific “sustainable fuel surcharge,” the net effect is that flexible and last-minute tickets to popular European sun destinations are incorporating a growing share of decarbonisation-related costs.

Some long-haul operators are experimenting with voluntary “green fares” that include higher blends of sustainable aviation fuel, marketed to environmentally conscious travelers. Early booking patterns suggest that while these products remain a niche, they can command a premium when paired with loyalty incentives, hinting at a future in which environmental attributes become a more visible differentiator in a crowded summer market.

Destinations Face Shifting Demand and Higher Access Costs

The economic reality for destinations is also changing. The European Commission’s own research highlights how climate change is likely to shift tourism demand within Europe over coming decades, with hotter southern coastal regions expected to lose some summer visitors while cooler northern and Atlantic regions gain. Overlaying this climate-driven redistribution with rising access costs from sustainable aviation fuel mandates creates a complex picture for local tourism economies.

Southern beach destinations that rely heavily on seasonal, price-sensitive air travel may be particularly exposed. Industry briefings note that budget airlines serving Mediterranean resorts are among the most reliant on high aircraft utilization and low unit costs. Higher fuel-related expenses during peak months could encourage a tilt toward higher-spending segments or longer average stays, as local authorities seek to preserve revenue even if total arrivals plateau.

At the same time, cities and regions that are reachable by rail or car from major emission-regulated hubs may see relative gains as travelers recalculate the cost of a summer break. Tourism boards in parts of central and northern Europe have already begun to emphasize “low-carbon access” by high-speed rail in their promotional campaigns, positioning themselves as both climate conscious and cost competitive as flying becomes structurally more expensive.

For small island destinations within the EU that depend almost entirely on air links, sustainable aviation fuel mandates pose a different challenge. Policy papers and industry submissions have warned that uniform blending obligations could disproportionately impact remote regions with no practical modal alternatives, prompting debate over whether complementary funding or targeted exemptions will be needed to safeguard connectivity without undermining climate objectives.

Pressure Builds on Suppliers and Policy Makers

The success of the EU’s sustainable aviation fuel push ultimately hinges on the pace at which supply can scale and costs can fall. Industry associations representing airlines have repeatedly argued that current global production volumes are far below what will be required to meet the 2 percent European mandate in the middle of this decade, let alone higher targets further out. Estimates circulated by these groups suggest that sustainable aviation fuel may cover well under 1 percent of global jet fuel demand in 2025.

Fuel producers, by contrast, point to new projects, refinery conversions and feedstock partnerships as evidence that capacity is ramping up. Communications from refining and biofuel industry groups in 2024 and 2025 stress that existing and planned facilities could already cover the initial 2 percent requirement across the EU, and that with predictable policy frameworks the sector is positioned to exceed the 2030 targets.

Policy design remains central. Alongside the ReFuelEU mandates, other elements of the EU’s Fit for 55 climate package, including reforms to the emissions trading system and temporary tax exemptions for sustainable aviation fuel, are intended to support uptake while avoiding abrupt shocks to connectivity. Academic analyses of environmental policy spillovers in tourism suggest that coordinated measures at origin and destination can moderate negative demand impacts and even enhance a region’s appeal among travelers who value visible climate action.

By 2027, the European Commission is required to review the aviation fuel regulation and assess whether the trajectory of blending targets, definitions and support mechanisms remains appropriate. That review is expected to weigh evidence from the first summers under the mandate, including how ticket prices, airline finances and tourism flows have evolved in practice.

Travelers Confront a New Price and Climate Equation

For travelers planning European summer holidays, the immediate manifestation of sustainable aviation fuel mandates is subtle but real. Comparison tools and booking platforms tracking fares into major EU leisure gateways show that average prices in recent peak seasons have remained above pre-pandemic levels, even as capacity has largely recovered. Analysts attribute part of that persistence to higher fuel and environmental compliance costs, alongside strong demand and constrained airport infrastructure.

Surveys of European consumers conducted by tourism research institutes in 2024 and 2025 suggest a growing willingness to factor environmental impact into travel decisions, particularly among younger cohorts. However, price remains the dominant driver for most households, especially when choosing between competing sun-and-sea destinations. The emerging pattern appears to be one of trade-offs: some travelers are flying less often but staying longer, others are opting for closer-to-home trips accessible by rail, while a segment of higher-income visitors is prepared to pay a premium for lower-carbon air travel options.

Travel industry forecasts indicate that the overall volume of summer tourism in Europe is likely to keep rising over the next decade, but with a shift in its economic geography and spending profile. As sustainable aviation fuel mandates ratchet up, the cost of getting to Europe’s beaches and cities may continue to climb gradually, even if airlines and fuel producers succeed in narrowing today’s price gap between green and conventional jet fuel.

For destinations, airlines and travelers alike, the new fuel rules mark the start of a long structural adjustment rather than a short-term shock. The way Europe manages this transition over successive summer seasons will help determine whether the world’s most visited region can remain both globally accessible and aligned with its climate ambitions.

title: How geopolitical tension in the Middle East is impacting business aviation
description:
Subscribe With around 700 business jets based in the Middle East, the region has experienced a sharp decline in activity amid rising geopolitical tensions.
Globally, the Middle East accounts for just 2% of business jet flights, compared with around 5% of commercial scheduled airline operations.
Rise in business charters from the Middle East WingX’s data also shows that most business aviation flights in the Middle East currently remain within the region.
So, the two are not entirely unconnected.” Long-term outlook remains stable Despite short-term volatility, the long-term outlook for business aviation remains steady.
However, Koe cautioned that geopolitical tensions will continue to affect business aviation in 2026 beyond the Middle East.
text:
Sign up for our newsletter and get our latest content in your inbox.

Subscribe

With around 700 business jets based in the Middle East, the region has experienced a sharp decline in activity amid rising geopolitical tensions.

Speaking at the British Business and General Aviation Association (BBGA) AGM on Thursday 12 March, Richard Koe, Managing Director of aviation data specialist WingX, explained that recent data across the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Turkey, and, to some extent, Israel shows that Turkey currently hosts the largest population of business jets in the region.

“In the initial days of the conflict in Iran and across the region, we saw a drop in business aviation activity of around 26%,” said Koe. “At the same time, airline activity fell even further, dropping by around 52%.”

Koe noted that this pattern mirrors what was observed during the COVID pandemic, when aviation activity initially dropped but was then followed by a surge in business jet traffic in 2022.

“It’s perhaps not all that surprising,” explained Koe. “Commercial carriers are often grounded during periods of disruption, whereas business jets continue flying because they provide flexibility and point-to-point connectivity.”

Despite the regional downturn, it has not been a “dramatic decline,” and activity has not stopped completely. Looking at year-to-date figures, Koe revealed that business jet traffic out of Israel is slightly up, while across the Middle East overall it is currently down around 5% compared to the start of the year. He also noted that some activity is still being recorded in Iran, primarily involving smaller jets.

Globally, the Middle East accounts for just 2% of business jet flights, compared with around 5% of commercial scheduled airline operations.

Rise in business charters from the Middle East

WingX’s data also shows that most business aviation flights in the Middle East currently remain within the region. However, Koe said there has also been a recent increase in charter activity from the region to Europe. “Over the past week, we have seen a pick-up in private charters from airports in the Middle East back to Europe, particularly the UK, France and Greece,” he said. “This is likely repatriation traffic, but also aircraft being repositioned away from potential threats.”

WingX has also been monitoring aircraft parked on the ground for customers. “It’s important to consider the representative value of these aircraft,” Koe said. At one point in early March, aircraft worth an estimated US$4.9 billion were parked in parts of the Middle East. In Dubai alone, 51 business jets were recorded on the ground on 3 March, although that number has since dropped to a handful as aircraft have been repositioned.

Impact of rising oil costs

Oil prices are another factor affecting the sector. “We’ve all seen the headlines with fuel pricing rising,” Koe said. “This will inevitably affect the aviation industry and could spill over into the wider economy.”

Fuel accounts for a large portion of operating costs in aviation. For business jets, it accounts for roughly 34% of operating costs, compared with about 25% for commercial airlines. Koe referenced a comment from the CEO of Hong Kong-based Beta Aviation, who revealed that a 30% increase in fuel prices could take out as much as 50% of profit margins.

Looking back over the past two decades, Koe explained that the relationship between oil prices and business jet activity is not always direct. However, there are notable moments where the two align. “In 2008, the last time oil prices were extremely high, we saw a steep decline in business jet activity, but also a recession. So, the two are not entirely unconnected.”

Long-term outlook remains stable

Despite short-term volatility, the long-term outlook for business aviation remains steady. While business jet deliveries have slowed after a surge during the pandemic recovery, this is not uncommon. The market has historically moved in cycles of strong growth followed by periods of consolidation.

“As the industry matures, these swings may become less pronounced,” Koe said, noting that forecasts suggest more stable growth of around 2–3% annually over the coming years.

The global distribution of the business jet fleet has also remained remarkably stable over time. The US continues to dominate the sector. Around 15 years ago, approximately 66% of the global fleet was based in the US. By 2025, that figure had risen slightly to 67%.

The most significant relative growth has been in Asia, where the fleet has doubled in size over the same period, although WingX’s data shows it still represents only around 6% of the global total.

Longevity is another key characteristic of the sector. Of the business jets delivered 50 years ago, around one-third remain in operation today. According to Koe, more than 350 Learjets delivered in the mid-1970s are still flying.

However, Koe cautioned that geopolitical tensions will continue to affect business aviation in 2026 beyond the Middle East. “Growth has plateaued during the first quarter of this year, but we are still seeing around 3% growth in business aviation traffic globally.” He concluded that with business jet deliveries worldwide up 14% and close to pre-covid 2019 figures in 2025, “It is very much a seller’s market.”

title: Jet Fuel Shock 2026: Why Global Airfares Are Climbing - The Traveler
description:
At the same time, airlines and airport operators in Europe are tracking a steady climb in average jet fuel prices.
In early March, coverage of a leading US airline’s investor event highlighted warnings that higher jet fuel prices were likely to feed through to airfares in the near term.
Reroutes, Longer Flights and Hidden Costs for Travelers The impact of the 2026 fuel shock is not limited to the price of jet fuel alone.
When conventional jet fuel prices climb at the same time, the combined effect can be particularly acute for transatlantic and intra-European itineraries.
What is increasingly clear is that the jet fuel crunch of early 2026 is reshaping the economics of global air travel in real time.
text:
Travelers booking spring and summer trips in 2026 are beginning to encounter a new kind of sticker shock, as a sudden spike in jet fuel prices collides with already strong demand for flights and forces airlines on nearly every continent to nudge fares higher.

A Perfect Storm in the Global Fuel Supply

The sharp rise in ticket prices unfolding this year is rooted far from airport check-in desks. In late February 2026, the conflict involving Iran and Western allies intensified, triggering a near shutdown of tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most important oil and gas chokepoints. Publicly available coverage of the crisis indicates that the waterway typically carries around a fifth of global crude and liquefied natural gas trade, making any prolonged closure a direct shock to fuel markets.

Oil markets reacted quickly. International benchmarks such as Brent crude have climbed into territory not seen since mid 2024, while separate reporting shows crude briefly topping 100 dollars per barrel in early March as traders priced in a risk of extended supply disruption. Jet fuel, refined from the same barrel of oil, has followed suit, with industry analyses highlighting a rapid jump from pre-crisis levels and warning of heightened volatility in the weeks ahead.

Energy-focused research from industry groups and analytics firms points to particularly steep increases in Asia and Europe, where refineries and airports rely heavily on imported crude and refined products that normally pass through the Gulf. A recent note from one aviation-focused research provider cited Singapore jet fuel prices surging more than 70 percent as Middle East supply lines were disrupted, underlining how quickly regional markets can tighten when a single strategic corridor is choked.

At the same time, airlines and airport operators in Europe are tracking a steady climb in average jet fuel prices. A March 2026 overview from a major European air traffic data provider reported jet fuel at 2.45 dollars per gallon at the end of February, a seven percent rise in just two weeks and the highest level since July 2024. Analysts caution that these numbers reflect wholesale prices and could move even higher if the conflict drags on into the peak summer travel period.

From Fuel Tanks to Ticket Prices

For airlines, jet fuel is typically the single largest operating expense, often accounting for 20 to 25 percent of total costs in normal conditions. When prices jump as rapidly as they have in early 2026, even well-managed carriers can struggle to absorb the increase. Industry commentary from aviation finance specialists notes that global airlines were expected to post net profit margins of under 4 percent this year, leaving little room to absorb a sustained fuel shock without passing some of the burden to passengers.

Recent public statements and earnings updates from major carriers underline the pressure. In early March, coverage of a leading US airline’s investor event highlighted warnings that higher jet fuel prices were likely to feed through to airfares in the near term. Analysts quoted in business media have echoed that assessment, suggesting that unless oil and jet fuel retreat quickly, many airlines will need to raise base fares or add fuel-related surcharges to protect already thin margins.

Official statistics also point to rising costs. The United States Bureau of Transportation Statistics reported this month that average aviation fuel cost per gallon for US airlines rose in January 2026 compared with December 2025, even before the latest escalation in the Middle East. While some carriers hedge a portion of their fuel needs, locking in prices through financial contracts, those positions typically cover only part of their consumption and often roll off over time, limiting their ability to fully shield customers from market shocks.

Some airlines, particularly in Asia, are already adjusting surcharges. Japan Airlines, for example, announced higher international fuel surcharges for tickets issued between February and March 2026, citing benchmark price movements that pushed it into a higher surcharge band under its regulatory framework. Industry news outlets report that other carriers in the region are reviewing similar steps as fuel benchmarks climb.

Reroutes, Longer Flights and Hidden Costs for Travelers

The impact of the 2026 fuel shock is not limited to the price of jet fuel alone. Airspace restrictions and safety considerations linked to the Iran war have forced airlines to reroute many long-haul services, particularly flights connecting Europe with Asia and Oceania that would normally rely on shorter, more direct corridors. Publicly available route-tracking data and airline advisories show extended detours around closed or high-risk airspace across parts of the Middle East.

These revised routings add flight time and fuel burn. Aviation consultants cited in recent industry analyses estimate that detours of two to three hours can add several thousand dollars in extra operating costs per flight, depending on the aircraft type and load factor. For heavily traveled corridors, the cumulative impact across an entire schedule can quickly run into tens of millions of dollars, giving airlines another incentive to adjust fares.

There are also insurance and operational costs tied to conflict zones. Travel industry explainers published in recent days highlight how war-risk insurance premiums for flights operating near Iran and neighboring states have risen sharply or, in some cases, become difficult to secure at previous rates. Carriers that suspend services must also contend with revenue losses, while those that continue to operate on modified routings face higher crew and maintenance expenses linked to longer sector times.

For travelers, these factors can show up in ways that are not always obvious. Instead of a clearly labeled “fuel surcharge,” airlines may adjust the underlying base fare or reduce the number of promotional seats on popular routes. Some carriers may also trim frequencies or deploy smaller aircraft on marginally profitable routes to reduce exposure to high fuel costs, leading to fewer seats in the market and added upward pressure on prices.

Regional Differences: Who Feels the Pinch First?

Not all travelers will experience the fuel-driven price shock in the same way or at the same time. Aviation analysts point out that carriers with robust fuel hedging programs, diversified route networks and strong balance sheets may be able to delay or soften fare increases. Others, particularly smaller or heavily indebted airlines, may need to move faster to protect their finances.

Asia-Pacific passengers are among the most exposed. Economic research on the region repeatedly underscores its reliance on imported fuel that normally transits the Strait of Hormuz. Reports from regional energy centers note that when supplies tighten, wealthier economies often outbid poorer ones for limited cargoes, amplifying price spikes for smaller markets. Airlines in these countries face the dual challenge of higher input costs and customers who are more sensitive to even modest fare hikes.

In Europe, where demand for long-haul leisure and business travel has rebounded strongly since the pandemic, airlines already contend with environmental charges and sustainable aviation fuel mandates that add to total fuel bills. Trade publications covering the sector describe how compliance with emerging SAF requirements has introduced extra costs, as limited supplies command premium prices. When conventional jet fuel prices climb at the same time, the combined effect can be particularly acute for transatlantic and intra-European itineraries.

North American travelers may see a more gradual effect. While US airlines are not immune to global oil shocks, they benefit from access to large domestic energy markets and, in some cases, more extensive hedging programs. Nevertheless, with official US data already recording higher fuel costs per gallon in early 2026, consumer travel sites and fare trackers are beginning to flag upward trends on selected routes, especially for peak summer departure dates.

What Travelers Can Expect for 2026 Bookings

With uncertainty surrounding the duration of the Strait of Hormuz disruption and the broader Iran war, forecasting exact fare levels for the rest of 2026 is difficult. Energy analysts featured in financial and commodity market coverage describe a wide range of possible price paths for oil and jet fuel, depending on how quickly shipping lanes normalize and whether other major producers maintain or adjust planned output increases in the coming months.

Even so, most publicly available aviation and travel sector commentary points toward at least a near-term period of elevated airfares. Industry news reports suggest that airlines are planning targeted price increases on longer, fuel-intensive routes, such as intercontinental services linking Europe with Asia-Pacific and North America with the Middle East. Travelers looking at these itineraries for late spring and summer may face higher starting prices and fewer deep-discount deals than in 2025.

For cost-conscious passengers, travel advisors and consumer groups recommend a familiar mix of strategies: booking earlier for peak travel periods, remaining flexible on dates and airports, and considering one-stop itineraries where nonstops have become significantly more expensive. Monitoring fare trends with price alerts and being prepared to adjust travel plans if geopolitical conditions worsen can also help mitigate the impact.

What is increasingly clear is that the jet fuel crunch of early 2026 is reshaping the economics of global air travel in real time. Unless geopolitical tensions ease and shipping lanes reopen fully, the higher costs now rippling through refineries, fuel depots and airline balance sheets are likely to keep airfares elevated, turning the simple act of booking a flight into a more expensive proposition for millions of travelers worldwide.

title: How Sustainable Fuel Mandates are Redefining the Economic Reality of Global Summer ...
description:
The Emergence of Environmental Surcharges A major transformation is being witnessed in how ticket prices are calculated, primarily due to the introduction of environmental levies.
These fees are being utilized to offset the immense costs associated with the transition to sustainable aviation fuel.
Within this package, the ReFuelEU Aviation regulation is specifically designed to increase both the demand for and supply of sustainable aviation fuels.
Because the infrastructure for large-scale production of sustainable fuel is still in its infancy, the procurement costs for airlines remain volatile.
This interconnectedness of the global aviation network means that few long-haul routes remain completely insulated from the rising costs.
text:
The landscape of global tourism is currently being reshaped by significant shifts in the aviation industry’s pricing structures. For those planning international excursions, the rising airfare costs and the implementation of new environmental surcharges have become central considerations for the upcoming season. It is observed that summer holiday budgets are being stretched as the aviation industry adapts to stringent new regulations designed to mitigate the environmental impact of long-haul flights. The European Union’s green initiatives are particularly noted as a primary driver behind these adjustments, influencing how carriers calculate the base cost of travel.

The Emergence of Environmental Surcharges

A major transformation is being witnessed in how ticket prices are calculated, primarily due to the introduction of environmental levies. It is reported that several major carriers, including the Lufthansa Group and Air France-KLM, have initiated the application of surcharges ranging from small increments to significant amounts per ticket. These fees are being utilized to offset the immense costs associated with the transition to sustainable aviation fuel. This fuel, while more environmentally friendly, is currently produced in limited quantities and at a much higher price point than traditional kerosene.

The decision to pass these costs onto the consumer is presented as a necessary step for the survival of the industry under new legislative frameworks. These frameworks, such as the ReFuelEU Aviation initiative, mandate that a specific percentage of fuel used by aircraft departing from European airports must consist of sustainable alternatives. Consequently, the financial burden of this ecological transition is being shared with passengers, leading to a noticeable inflation in the cost of mid-to-long-range flights.

Impact on Global Vacation Planning

The traditional approach to planning a summer getaway is being re-evaluated by travelers worldwide. It is observed that the affordability of popular destinations is being compromised by these sudden price hikes. For individuals traveling from regions such as India to various European hubs, the cumulative effect of these surcharges, when multiplied across a family or group, can be substantial. The era of low-cost, long-haul travel is perceived to be under threat as the true cost of carbon emissions is integrated into the commercial aviation model.

Furthermore, the timing of these increases coincides with a period of high demand. As families attempt to secure bookings for the peak summer months, they are met with a market where base fares are already elevated due to inflation and high fuel prices. The addition of a “green fee” is viewed as an additional layer of complexity that necessitates a more rigorous approach to financial planning. It is suggested that many travelers are now opting for shorter distances or domestic destinations to avoid the heavy surcharges associated with intercontinental routes.

Regulatory Frameworks and the ReFuelEU Initiative

The legislative backbone of these changes is found within the European Union’s Fit for 55 package. This policy aims to reduce net greenhouse gas emissions by at least 55 percent by the year 2030. Within this package, the ReFuelEU Aviation regulation is specifically designed to increase both the demand for and supply of sustainable aviation fuels. By the year 2025, it is mandated that at least 2 percent of aviation fuel must be sustainable, with this figure rising incrementally to 70 percent by 2050.

The logistical challenges of meeting these quotas are immense. Because the infrastructure for large-scale production of sustainable fuel is still in its infancy, the procurement costs for airlines remain volatile. It is argued by industry experts that without these surcharges, the financial stability of major airlines would be jeopardized. Therefore, the narrative provided by the carriers focuses on the necessity of these fees to fund the research and development required for a carbon-neutral future in the skies.

Regional Variations in Ticket Pricing

The impact of these surcharges is not uniform across the globe. While European carriers are at the forefront of this movement due to regional laws, the ripple effects are felt by international partners and competitors alike. For instance, a flight booked through a codeshare agreement might still carry these levies even if the primary carrier is not based in Europe. This interconnectedness of the global aviation network means that few long-haul routes remain completely insulated from the rising costs.

In addition to the fuel-related fees, other factors such as increased ground handling charges and airport taxes are being noted. In some jurisdictions, carbon taxes are also being applied at the national level, further compounding the expense for the traveler. It is observed that the cumulative effect of these various line items is a significant departure from the transparent pricing models of previous decades. Travelers are encouraged to scrutinize the breakdown of their fare to understand the various components contributing to the final price.

Future Outlook for the Travel Industry

As the aviation sector continues its journey toward decarbonization, the trend of rising surcharges is expected to persist. It is anticipated that other regions beyond Europe may adopt similar mandates as global pressure to address climate change intensifies. This suggests that the current pricing environment is not a temporary spike but rather a structural shift in the economics of air travel.

The resilience of the travel market is being tested by these developments. While the desire for international exploration remains strong, the frequency and duration of trips may be adjusted by the average consumer. A shift toward more sustainable travel habits, such as choosing direct flights to reduce emissions or staying longer in a single destination rather than hopping between cities, is being encouraged by environmental advocates. In the long term, the successful integration of sustainable fuels may lead to a more stable pricing environment, but the transition period is characterized by financial uncertainty for both airlines and their passengers.

Recommendations for Budget-Conscious Travelers

In light of these escalating costs, several strategies are being employed by savvy travelers to mitigate the impact on their holiday budgets. Booking significantly in advance remains a primary recommendation, as it allows passengers to lock in fares before seasonal demand drives them higher. Additionally, flexibility with travel dates and the use of secondary airports can often result in lower base fares, which helps offset the mandatory surcharges.

The use of loyalty programs and travel rewards is also being highlighted as a way to dampen the blow of increased ticket prices. By leveraging points for upgrades or fare discounts, the out-of-pocket expense for the environmental levies can be balanced. Ultimately, a new level of financial transparency is being demanded by consumers, who wish to see exactly how their contributions are being utilized to foster a greener aviation industry. As the industry moves forward, the balance between environmental responsibility and the accessibility of global travel will remain a central theme in the narrative of modern tourism.

title: New Era Cleantech starts ₹20000 crore coal gasification in Chandrapur
description:
Ltd today announced starting work on India’s first greenfield integrated coal gasification and carbon capture complex at Bhadrawati in Maharashtra’s Chandrapur, with an investment of ₹20,000 crore.
Spread across around 1,650 acres the complex will convert domestic coal into key industrial chemicals and fuels used in sectors such as fertilisers, mining, textiles and packaging.
Once operational, the facility is expected to process over 5 million metric tonnes of coal annually.
Future phases are expected to expand the product slate to include dimethyl ether (DME), urea, sustainable aviation fuel and ethanol.
According to the company, converting India’s abundant coal reserves into industrial inputs could help reduce dependence on imported chemicals, fertilisers and fuels while strengthening the country’s long-term energy security.
text:
New Era Cleantech Solutions Pvt. Ltd today announced starting work on India’s first greenfield integrated coal gasification and carbon capture complex at Bhadrawati in Maharashtra’s Chandrapur, with an investment of ₹20,000 crore.

Spread across around 1,650 acres the complex will convert domestic coal into key industrial chemicals and fuels used in sectors such as fertilisers, mining, textiles and packaging. Once operational, the facility is expected to process over 5 million metric tonnes of coal annually.

In its first phase, the project will produce ammonia, ammonium nitrate and mono ethylene glycol. Future phases are expected to expand the product slate to include dimethyl ether (DME), urea, sustainable aviation fuel and ethanol.

The project was first cleared by the Maharashtra government in 2022 and is positioned as part of India’s push for industrial self-reliance under the Atmanirbhar Bharat initiative. The project also comes at a time when global gas supplies have been disrupted amid tensions involving Iran and constraints around the Strait of Hormuz, a key route for global LNG trade.

According to the company, converting India’s abundant coal reserves into industrial inputs could help reduce dependence on imported chemicals, fertilisers and fuels while strengthening the country’s long-term energy security.

Published on March 14, 2026

title: South Dakota Senate Declines To Put Eminent Domain Restrictions On The Ballot
authors: Makenzie Huber for South Dakota Searchlight, Meghan O'Brien, South Dakota Searchlight, Jeff Beach, North Dakota Monitor
description:
South Dakota voters will not be asked to put eminent domain restrictions in the state constitution, the state Senate decided.
Eminent domain is the power to take private property for public use, with just compensation to the landowner.
Sen. Joy Hohn, R-Hartford, was an outspoken critic of eminent domain use for the carbon capture pipeline, which was proposed by Iowa-based Summit Carbon Solutions.
Hohn supported the ban on eminent domain for carbon pipelines last year, and it was a top issue in her 2024 Senate campaign.
The project has been granted permits in other states, but those have been subjected to legal challenges, and South Dakota regulators have twice denied permits for the project.
text:
South Dakota voters will not be asked to put eminent domain restrictions in the state constitution, the state Senate decided.

Supporters of House Joint Resolution 5001, which failed 14-19 earlier this month, had argued that residents need stronger protections than those found in last year’s law banning eminent domain for carbon capture pipelines.

Eminent domain is the power to take private property for public use, with just compensation to the landowner. Although eminent domain is often used for water pipelines, electrical transmission lines, and other infrastructure, it’s been a divisive issue in South Dakota the last several years because of a proposed multi-state carbon capture pipeline that would pass through the eastern part of the state.

The resolution would have asked voters to narrow the use of eminent domain. Increases in the tax base, tax revenues, employment, or general economic health would no longer justify its use, and the need for a project would need to be publicly declared before any condemnation proceedings could commence.

Sen. Joy Hohn, R-Hartford, was an outspoken critic of eminent domain use for the carbon capture pipeline, which was proposed by Iowa-based Summit Carbon Solutions. Hohn supported the ban on eminent domain for carbon pipelines last year, and it was a top issue in her 2024 Senate campaign.

Hohn’s property has been condemned twice by companies running pipeline projects through the state, the first being the Dakota Access oil pipeline, the second being Summit.

“Different products, different politics, same reality: private companies pursuing profit were allowed, at least initially, to initiate condemnation proceedings against our land,” she said.

Hohn was nonetheless one of 19 senators to vote against the resolution, which had previously passed the House 62-5.

She raised concerns about water and electric utility projects, among others.

“We must assure ourselves that what we propose is the very best we can offer the people of South Dakota,” Hohn said, adding, “I am not convinced this is the best we can do.”

MORE: Rural ambulance, emergency challenges come to forefront during legislative coffee session

Sen. Mark Lapka, R-Long Lake, sponsored the resolution in the Senate. He told lawmakers that case law establishes water and electric projects as a public need.

Summit’s proposed five-state pipeline would capture carbon dioxide from ethanol plants in South Dakota, North Dakota, Minnesota, Iowa and Nebraska and transport it to an underground sequestration site in North Dakota, to capitalize on federal tax credits incentivizing the prevention of heat-trapping gases into the atmosphere. The project has been granted permits in other states, but those have been subjected to legal challenges, and South Dakota regulators have twice denied permits for the project.

Lapka, Sen. Mike Rohl, R-Aberdeen, and Sen. Carl Perry, R-Aberdeen each voted yes.

title: Biofuel industry awaits Commonwealth support as fuel tensions increase in Middle East
authors: Daniel Miles, Justine Longmore, David Claughton, Warwick Long, www.abc.net.au, daniel-miles, justine-longmore, david-claughton, warwick-long
description:
Not because he's hankering for a pot and a parma, but because the cooking oil from the kitchen is what fills the tank of his converted farm ute.
He buys used cooking oil from restaurants near his 4-hectare timber farm in Kawarren, near the Otway Ranges in south-west Victoria, which he refines and stores on site.
With petrol prices surging as a result of the ongoing war in the Middle East, Mr Woodcroft said his alternative fuel had become a financial and environmental lifeline.
The biofuel industry argues it's an environmentally friendly fuel source that would boost jobs and firm up Australia's fuel supply without ripping up the infrastructure already in place.
A September 2024 report from the Clean Energy Finance Corporation estimated Australia's low-carbon liquid fuel industry could be worth $36 billion by 2050.
text:
When Terry Woodcroft needs to fill up his trusty Toyota Hilux, he heads straight to the local pub.

Not because he's hankering for a pot and a parma, but because the cooking oil from the kitchen is what fills the tank of his converted farm ute.

Mr Woodcroft is a retired police officer and hobby farmer who converted his ute about five years ago to run on biofuel, such as vegetable oil.

He buys used cooking oil from restaurants near his 4-hectare timber farm in Kawarren, near the Otway Ranges in south-west Victoria, which he refines and stores on site.

With petrol prices surging as a result of the ongoing war in the Middle East, Mr Woodcroft said his alternative fuel had become a financial and environmental lifeline.

"It certainly makes quite a dent in the fuel bill, I have to admit that," he said.

The conversion is driven by equal parts environment and economy.

He believes everyone should do what they can to reduce their footprint: the hip-pocket benefit is a silver lining.

"I am worried about the future in a way I have never been before," he said.

It's also helped him gather something of a unique reputation around town.

"Friends make out that they can smell it coming because it smells like fish and chips cooking," he said.

"It does — it doesn't smell like a diesel car."

What is biofuel?

Biofuel is made from organic waste products, including grains, forestry material and agricultural waste, and even used cooking oil from restaurants and takeaway shops.

It can be used in existing petrol and diesel engines with little to no modification, which is part of what makes it attractive to farmers like Mr Woodcroft.

The biofuel industry argues it's an environmentally friendly fuel source that would boost jobs and firm up Australia's fuel supply without ripping up the infrastructure already in place.

"Ultimately, Australia really does need to consider how we operate, not only from an emissions perspective, but from the fuel security perspective," Bioenergy Australia chief executive Shahana McKenzie said.

Ian O'Hara, deputy dean of the Faculty of Engineering at Queensland University of Technology, said bolstering the biofuel market would lead to more regional jobs and better fuel security for Australians.

"Twenty years ago in Australia, we produced 80 per cent of the fuel we needed. Now we import 80 per cent," he said.

Professor O'Hara said the industry needed federal government support to become a commercial reality.

A September 2024 report from the Clean Energy Finance Corporation estimated Australia's low-carbon liquid fuel industry could be worth $36 billion by 2050.

Last year, the government established a $1.1 billion fund to stimulate private investment in the low-carbon fuels sector.

"Our focus is on building Australian supply, Australian refining capability and stronger fuel resilience," a spokesperson for Energy Minister Chris Bowen said.

How much biofuel do we already produce?

Australia has the ability to produce up to 110 million litres of biofuel per year, but produces just a fraction of that.

Instead, much of the raw products are exported to other nations to be turned into biofuel overseas.

The nation's largest plant at Barnawartha, in Victoria's north-east, is running at 20 per cent of its 60 million litre potential.

Just BioDiesel director Peter Chomley said it was too expensive to do otherwise.

"Of course, we'd like that to be at 100 per cent of capacity," he said.

The cost of making the fuel, he said, was about $2.20 per litre, which is more than the price of mineral diesel.

"There are subsidies in the EU and the US that support renewable fuels, and that's pushed the price of the [raw materials] up over the years," he said.

Australia exported nearly 6 million tonnes of canola from 2023–2024 to be turned into biofuel overseas.

Mr Chomley wants the government to put $500 million towards a 50-cent per litre subsidy, which could boost production to 1 billion litres.

"That's the sort of level of subsidy [needed] to build a significant industry," he said.

Andrea Polson from bioenergy producer Licella would like to see a country-wide mandate on biofuel use.

"[Mandates] do help build capacity and bring that price down," she said.

"In Australia, renewable diesel, like other renewable fuels, is quite a small industry from a capacity standpoint.

"As capacity builds, as the industry develops and matures, the price will come down."

outlet: Phillips 66 Stock (ISIN: US74460D1090) Faces Volatility Amid Mixed Earnings and Analyst Upgrades
title: Phillips 66 Stock (ISIN: US74460D1090) Faces Volatility Amid Mixed Earnings and Analyst Upgrades
description:
Q4 2025 Earnings: Beats on EPS, Misses on Revenue Phillips 66 reported Q4 2025 earnings that beat estimates by 9.78% at $2.47 per share, though revenue fell short by 5.86%.
Projected EPS growth of 74.26% to $11.85 drives appeal, though revenue volatility tempers views.
Business Model: Refining, Midstream, and Beyond Phillips 66 differentiates through downstream focus: refining (core), midstream (pipelines, logistics), chemicals, and renewables.
European and DACH Investor Perspective For German, Austrian, and Swiss investors, Phillips 66 stock (ISIN: US74460D1090) trades on Xetra, offering CHF or EUR hedging against USD oil exposure.
Sector Context and Competitive Positioning In oil refining, Phillips 66 competes with Valero and Marathon, boasting top-tier complexity (Nelson Index).
text:
Phillips 66 stock (ISIN: US74460D1090), the NYSE-listed ordinary shares of the Houston-based diversified energy manufacturing and logistics firm, closed lower on March 13, 2026, amid mixed quarterly results and sector headwinds. The stock fell 0.78% with 45% lower trading volume, contrasting its 4.95% monthly gain against the S&P 500's 2.25% decline. This pullback highlights ongoing tensions between robust earnings delivery and revenue pressures in refining.

As of: 14.03.2026

By Elena Voss, Senior Energy Markets Analyst - Specializing in US midstream and refining dynamics for European investors.

Current Market Snapshot and Trading Dynamics

Phillips 66, trading as PSX on the NYSE, operates as an independent refiner with midstream, chemicals, and renewables segments, distinct from integrated oil majors. On March 13, 2026, shares slid amid lower volume, trading around levels consistent with recent forecasts near $174. The 52-week range spans $91.01 to $142.35 as of late 2025 data, with recent prices reflecting upward momentum before the dip.

Technical indicators show the stock in a short-term rising trend, with potential 17.57% upside over three months to $144.71-$162.38, though a break below $123.08 could signal reversal. Volatility remains medium at 2.29% weekly, with support at Fibonacci levels like $123.29. For DACH investors accessing via Xetra, liquidity ties to NYSE but offers euro-denominated exposure to US refining cycles.

Q4 2025 Earnings: Beats on EPS, Misses on Revenue

Phillips 66 reported Q4 2025 earnings that beat estimates by 9.78% at $2.47 per share, though revenue fell short by 5.86%. Trailing twelve-month metrics include EPS of $4.16, net margins of 1.27%, and return on equity at 4.87%. Annual sales reached $145.50 billion, with cash flow at $12.14 per share.

Refining margins face pressure from fluctuating crude prices, a core driver for Phillips 66's midstream and downstream operations. The company's debt-to-equity ratio of 0.60 and current ratio of 1.07 signal solid balance sheet health amid capex for midstream investments. Institutional selling of 3.8-4.8% stakes ($159M-$35.9M) added downward pressure, offset by a 5.8% dividend hike yielding 3.1%.

Analyst Sentiment Shifts to Cautious Optimism

Analysts maintain a Moderate Buy consensus with a 2.59 rating score from 7 buys, 12 holds, and price target of $141.50, implying 4.8% upside. Recent upgrades include Wall Street Zen to Strong Buy and Morgan Stanley lifting target to $147 from $140 with Equal Weight. Forward P/E at 14.66 (7.1% below industry) and PEG of 0.59 (vs. 2.7 industry) suggest undervaluation.

Projected EPS growth of 74.26% to $11.85 drives appeal, though revenue volatility tempers views. Eleven Buy and twelve Hold ratings point to median target $160.11. For European investors, this positions PSX as a value play in energy transition, with less upstream exposure than peers.

Business Model: Refining, Midstream, and Beyond

Phillips 66 differentiates through downstream focus: refining (core), midstream (pipelines, logistics), chemicals, and renewables. Unlike integrated majors, it lacks significant upstream, reducing commodity price volatility but tying fortunes to crack spreads and throughput. Midstream investments, like the Western Gateway Pipeline open season announced October 2025, bolster logistics.

Renewables growth via DCOOP joint venture targets sustainable aviation fuel, aligning with EU green mandates relevant for DACH portfolios. Operating leverage shines in high-margin refining cycles, with pretax margins at 1.60%. Cash conversion supports $5.08 annual dividend, paid quarterly, ex-date February 23, 2026.

European and DACH Investor Perspective

For German, Austrian, and Swiss investors, Phillips 66 stock (ISIN: US74460D1090) trades on Xetra, offering CHF or EUR hedging against USD oil exposure. Refining resilience matters amid Europe's energy security push post-Ukraine crisis, where US LNG and products fill gaps. Dividend yield of 3.55% appeals to yield-focused DACH funds, outperforming bund yields.

Risks include EU carbon border taxes impacting imports, but Phillips 66's renewables pivot mitigates. Compared to European peers like OMV, PSX offers purer refining play with higher ROE potential. Portfolio diversification via NYSE/Xetra access balances DAX energy weights.

Related reading

Key Drivers: Margins, Cash Flow, and Capital Allocation

Refining margins drive 70%+ of earnings, sensitive to WTI/Brent spreads and utilization. Q4 revenue miss underscores demand softness, but $33.77 billion quarterly sales beat prior expectations. Free cash flow funds buybacks and dividends, with book value $68.92 per share.

Capex prioritizes midstream for fee-based revenues, reducing cyclicality. Balance sheet strength (quick ratio 0.79) supports resilience. Dividend growth from $1.15 quarterly signals confidence, record date March 4, 2026. Trade-off: higher payouts limit aggressive renewables scale-up.

Sector Context and Competitive Positioning

In oil refining, Phillips 66 competes with Valero and Marathon, boasting top-tier complexity (Nelson Index). Midstream assets like Gray Oak pipeline yield stable cash. Sector faces EV transition, but jet fuel and diesel demand persists, especially in Europe.

Phillips 66's 1.11 beta reflects market sensitivity. Growth strategy emphasizes diversification: chemicals via CPChem JV, renewables ramp. Vs. integrateds like Exxon, lower upstream risk but higher refining volatility.

Catalysts, Risks, and Outlook

Catalysts include EPS upgrades, pipeline expansions, dividend hikes. Q1 2026 results could reaccelerate if crack spreads widen. Risks: crude volatility, regulatory shifts, insider/CFO sales (14.5-14.8% holdings cut).

Outlook favors moderate upside if refining stabilizes above 50-day MA $148.22. For DACH investors, PSX offers yield and growth in energy transition. Monitor Zacks revisions for short-term trends.

title: Taiwan's Mandarin Airlines takes delivery of 12th ATR from France | Mar. 14, 2026 19:51
description:
TAIPEI (Taiwan News) – Mandarin Airlines took delivery of its 12th ATR aircraft Saturday after a flight from France via Africa to Asia in order to avoid the war in the Middle East.
On its voyage, the ATR 72-600 left the manufacturing plant in Toulouse to fly across Egypt, East Africa, the Maldives, India, and Thailand, per CNA.
The aircraft landed at Taipei Songshan Airport at 3:30 p.m. Saturday after a trip taking eight days instead of the originally planned five days.
Mandarin Airlines used the ATR planes to ply domestic routes, mostly linking the outlying islands of Kinmen, Matsu, and Penghu to Taiwan’s main island.
The latest delivery also marked a milestone because the plane used a 30% Sustainable Aviation Fuel blend, Mandarin Airlines executives said.
text:
TAIPEI (Taiwan News) – Mandarin Airlines took delivery of its 12th ATR aircraft Saturday after a flight from France via Africa to Asia in order to avoid the war in the Middle East.

On its voyage, the ATR 72-600 left the manufacturing plant in Toulouse to fly across Egypt, East Africa, the Maldives, India, and Thailand, per CNA. The aircraft landed at Taipei Songshan Airport at 3:30 p.m. Saturday after a trip taking eight days instead of the originally planned five days.

Mandarin Airlines used the ATR planes to ply domestic routes, mostly linking the outlying islands of Kinmen, Matsu, and Penghu to Taiwan’s main island. They fly 11 routes between eight airports. A 13th ATR was expected to join the fleet in April.

The latest delivery also marked a milestone because the plane used a 30% Sustainable Aviation Fuel blend, Mandarin Airlines executives said. The engine was also upgraded to use less energy and cut carbon emissions, while a new type of air conditioning system was fitted to provide passengers with a cleaner and healthier environment.

title: Dave Hengel Column: Laying the foundation for growth and development - Bemidji Pioneer
description:
As we celebrate 40 years of service to the Greater Bemidji region, we at Greater Bemidji find ourselves at a pivotal moment.
Economic development is no longer as simple as offering cheap land and waiting for the phone to ring.
Our region’s economic growth continues to be strong and steady.
Empowering local business: We will serve as the regional hub for the Northwest Small Business Development Center and reengage our "Grow Bemidji" business retention and expansion initiative with the Bemidji Chamber.
Quality of life: Economic growth is meaningless if people don't want to live here.
text:
As we celebrate 40 years of service to the Greater Bemidji region, we at Greater Bemidji find ourselves at a pivotal moment. Economic development is no longer as simple as offering cheap land and waiting for the phone to ring. Today’s landscape is complex, requiring a proactive approach to the "essential building blocks" of prosperity: child care, housing, a workforce ready for the future and an exceptional quality of life.

We recently completed our 2025 Annual Report of progress. The CliffsNotes version of the report: (1) our region continues to grow in a slow, steady pace; (2) the result of Greater Bemidji’s work is clear and meaningful; (3) as an organization, Greater Bemidji is strong, focused and backed by solid trust from our local and state partners; and (4) we are committed to a plan of action that serves our region well.

The numbers tell a great story. Our region’s economic growth continues to be strong and steady. Over the last decade, our real GDP grew by 21.7%, adding $866 million to our economy. Real per capita income has increased by over 18%. We have added nearly 1,500 workers to the workforce and created nearly 1,600 new jobs. Nationally, Bemidji’s economy is now ranked 114th out of 543 micropolitans.

We still face challenges. The number of unemployed in our region is still high and our poverty rate is still about the state and national averages. We have both a workforce housing and child care shortage that is an obstacle to growth. But we are meeting these challenges head-on by investing in initiatives that help make this region work.

In 2025, Greater Bemidji’s efforts touched every corner of our business community:

Small business support: We aided 164 businesses, provided over 2,000 hours of technical assistance, and helped local firms access $3.3 million in capital. Notably, over 60% of these businesses were women, BIPOC or veteran-owned.

Entrepreneurship: Our LaunchPad supported 36 members monthly and aided in the startup of 41 new businesses in the region.

Placemaking: We funded three new childcare centers (one in Bemidji and two in Red Lake) and led efforts to redevelop the rail corridor downtown, including the construction of the region’s first YMCA.

Workforce development: Our training center — the Minnesota Innovation Initiative — partnered with 46 companies to train 170 workers, while our 218 Relocate initiative welcomed 17 new families to the area.

We aren't slowing down. As we look toward 2026, our strategy is clear: we will do whatever it takes to move this region forward.

Empowering local business: We will serve as the regional hub for the Northwest Small Business Development Center and reengage our "Grow Bemidji" business retention and expansion initiative with the Bemidji Chamber. By providing flexible financing and access to state and federal incentives, we will ensure our local companies have the "fuel" they need to expand.

Cultivating innovation: Through the Headwaters Angel Network, we will provide entrepreneurs with the risk capital necessary to turn big ideas into local jobs. The LaunchPad will remain a "one-stop shop" for every dreamer and doer in our community.

Strategic infrastructure and promotion: Infrastructure is the backbone of growth. We are pursuing the construction of a regional rail spur and developing a strategy to aggressively expand our manufacturing sector. Furthermore, we are actively engaged in the Minnesota Sustainable Aviation Fuel Hub, looking to use our region’s woody biomass to put Bemidji at the forefront of green energy.

Quality of life: Economic growth is meaningless if people don't want to live here. We remain committed to fully developing the YMCA and Rail Corridor, increasing workforce housing options, and creating a community where families can thrive for generations.

To be clear, this isn't a solo act — our success comes directly from the shared commitment and financial support of our local community. Together, we’re proving that Bemidji is Minnesota’s emerging regional center.

To everyone in our corner: thank you. We are better equipped than ever to drive growth and prosperity. Let’s keep building a region we’re all proud to call home.

title: Ola Electric launches 'End ICE Age' campaign to promote EV shift - BioEnergy Times
description:
Ola Electric has launched a nationwide campaign called #EndICEAge to encourage riders across India to move away from petrol-powered two-wheelers and adopt electric vehicles, The Economic Times reported.
The initiative highlights the rising cost of petrol and the inconvenience of frequent refuelling, urging consumers to shift from internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles to electric mobility.
As part of the campaign, the company is offering benefits worth over Rs 20,000 for customers purchasing a new Ola S1 scooter or Ola Roadster motorcycle.
The company has also introduced a social media initiative inviting new customers to share their switch from petrol to electric vehicles.
The limited Champions Edition models will be available at company stores and online until the end of April 2026.
text:
Ola Electric has launched a nationwide campaign called #EndICEAge to encourage riders across India to move away from petrol-powered two-wheelers and adopt electric vehicles, The Economic Times reported.

The initiative highlights the rising cost of petrol and the inconvenience of frequent refuelling, urging consumers to shift from internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles to electric mobility.

As part of the campaign, the company is offering benefits worth over Rs 20,000 for customers purchasing a new Ola S1 scooter or Ola Roadster motorcycle. The limited-period offer, available for three days until March 16, includes a Rs 10,000 cash discount and an extended eight-year battery warranty valued at up to Rs 15,000.

The company has also introduced a social media initiative inviting new customers to share their switch from petrol to electric vehicles. Buyers who purchase an Ola Electric two-wheeler between now and March 16 can upload their last month’s petrol bills on social media, tag the company and use the campaign hashtag.

According to the company, three selected participants each day will have their petrol expenses adjusted against the cost of their new electric vehicle purchase.

A company spokesperson said the campaign aims to encourage people to move away from petrol-based mobility and embrace electric transportation. The spokesperson added that the company’s electric scooters and motorcycles offer a cleaner and more economical commuting option.

The company recently unveiled the Champions Edition of its flagship Ola S1 Pro+ scooter and Ola Roadster X+ motorcycle, powered by its 4680 Bharat Cell. The special edition was introduced to celebrate India’s third title win at the ICC Men’s T20 World Cup.

The limited Champions Edition models will be available at company stores and online until the end of April 2026.

title: Bentley to use sustainable aviation fuel for airfreight of customer cars - BioEnergy Times
description:
Bentley Motors has announced that it will use Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) for all airfreight shipments of customer cars worldwide, a move aimed at cutting emissions from its global logistics operations, MEM Sustainability reported.
The decision takes effect immediately and forms part of the company’s wider efforts to reduce carbon emissions in its supply chain.
Bentley said air transport is used only in limited situations such as urgent deliveries or specific customer requirements, but when it is necessary the company will now rely on SAF instead of conventional aviation fuel.
Aimee Kelly, Head of Sustainability at Bentley Motors, said the shift to certified SAF reflects the company’s efforts to reduce emissions in areas where flights remain necessary.
Bentley said it has already transported several customer vehicles using SAF and recorded significant reductions in emissions compared with regular aviation fuel.
text:
Bentley Motors has announced that it will use Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) for all airfreight shipments of customer cars worldwide, a move aimed at cutting emissions from its global logistics operations, MEM Sustainability reported.

The decision takes effect immediately and forms part of the company’s wider efforts to reduce carbon emissions in its supply chain. Bentley said air transport is used only in limited situations such as urgent deliveries or specific customer requirements, but when it is necessary the company will now rely on SAF instead of conventional aviation fuel.

SAF is produced from renewable or waste-based sources and can be used in existing aircraft without any modifications. Depending on how it is produced, the fuel can lower lifecycle carbon emissions by as much as 70 to 95 per cent compared with traditional jet fuel.

Aimee Kelly, Head of Sustainability at Bentley Motors, said the shift to certified SAF reflects the company’s efforts to reduce emissions in areas where flights remain necessary.

Bentley said it has already transported several customer vehicles using SAF and recorded significant reductions in emissions compared with regular aviation fuel.

Currently, the use of SAF applies to all airfreight shipments of customer vehicles, while the company is also exploring the possibility of extending the fuel to other logistics operations that require air transport.

The initiative is part of Bentley’s broader sustainability programme under its Beyond100+ strategy, which aims to reduce emissions across the company’s operations and support its long-term goal of achieving net zero carbon emissions.

title: Energy Shockwaves: Travel Sector Reeling as Iran-Israel Conflict Sends Fuel Costs Skyward
description:
The sell-off reflects growing investor anxiety over surging operational costs and the potential for a prolonged disruption of critical global energy and transit routes.
With Brent Crude prices skyrocketing from $70 to over $120 per barrel in less than a fortnight, the thin profit margins of the travel industry are under immense pressure.
In the energy space, Exxon Mobil Corporation (NYSE: XOM) and Chevron Corporation (NYSE: CVX) have emerged as safe havens.
The Path Forward: Rerouting and Resilience In the short term, the travel industry must pivot toward survival strategies.
The era of "frictionless" global travel is being tested by the realities of kinetic warfare and energy blockades.
text:
The global travel and transport sectors are facing their most significant crisis since the 2020 pandemic as the escalation of a full-scale war between Iran and Israel has sent energy markets into a tailspin. On March 13, 2026, shares of major travel providers plummeted, led by sharp declines in United Airlines Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ: UAL), which fell 4.6%, and Carnival Corporation & plc (NYSE: CCL), which saw a staggering 7.9% drop. The sell-off reflects growing investor anxiety over surging operational costs and the potential for a prolonged disruption of critical global energy and transit routes.

The immediate implications of this geopolitical flare-up are being felt at the pump and in the cockpit. With Brent Crude prices skyrocketing from $70 to over $120 per barrel in less than a fortnight, the thin profit margins of the travel industry are under immense pressure. Analysts warn that if the conflict continues to simmer, the $22.6 billion in market value wiped from the travel sector in early March may only be the beginning of a broader structural decline for high-fuel-consumption industries.

A Flashpoint in the Middle East: From Escalation to Energy Crisis

The current market turmoil traces back to February 28, 2026, when a series of joint U.S. and Israeli air strikes, internally dubbed "Operation Epic Fury," targeted Iranian military infrastructure and air defense systems. The strikes, which reportedly led to the assassination of Iran’s Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei and the immediate succession of his son, Mojtaba Khamenei, triggered an unprecedented retaliatory response. Iran effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, a move that halted roughly 20% of the world’s seaborne oil supply and reduced tanker traffic from 60 ships per day to fewer than five.

As of March 13, 2026, the conflict has intensified, with U.S. Central Command reporting strikes on over 6,000 targets within Iran. In retaliation, drone and missile attacks have targeted oil infrastructure in neighboring Gulf states and Israeli population centers. This has forced major aviation hubs, such as Dubai International Airport (DXB), into multi-day closures, stranding tens of thousands of passengers and necessitating costly, long-distance rerouting for international carriers.

The speed of the fuel price surge has been historic. Jet fuel prices witnessed a 58% jump in a single week, reaching $3.95 per gallon by March 6. For carriers like United Airlines (NASDAQ: UAL), which traditionally does not hedge its fuel expenses, the impact was immediate. CEO Scott Kirby has already cautioned that the carrier’s first-quarter financial results will take a "meaningful" hit, as the airline is now fully exposed to the extreme volatility of the spot market.

Winners and Losers in a War-Torn Market

The travel sector is undoubtedly the primary victim of this energy shock. Carnival Corp (NYSE: CCL) has been hit twice over: once by the surging cost of marine diesel and again by the closure of vital maritime routes in the Middle East, forcing the cancellation of dozens of high-margin luxury cruises. Competitors like Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd. (NYSE: NCLH) and Royal Caribbean Group (NYSE: RCL) have seen similar losses, with shares sliding between 6% and 7% as the "revenge travel" boom of the mid-2020s hits a geopolitical brick wall.

Conversely, the defense and domestic energy sectors have seen a dramatic re-rating. Lockheed Martin Corporation (NYSE: LMT) shares reached an all-time high of $692.00 on the back of massive demand for the THAAD anti-ballistic system and F-35 aircraft. Similarly, RTX Corporation (NYSE: RTX), formerly Raytheon, saw its backlog swell to a record $268 billion as the conflict depleted global stockpiles of Tomahawk cruise missiles and Patriot defense systems.

In the energy space, Exxon Mobil Corporation (NYSE: XOM) and Chevron Corporation (NYSE: CVX) have emerged as safe havens. XOM surged to $154.00, benefiting from its "geopolitically safe" production in the Permian Basin and Guyana. Chevron’s exposure to the Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) supercycle has also made it a favorite for investors, particularly as Iranian drone strikes disrupted Qatari gas production, making North American exports more critical than ever to the global energy balance.

Broader Significance and Historical Echoes

The 2026 energy crisis bears a striking resemblance to the 1973 oil embargo and the 1990 Gulf War, but with the added complexity of a modern, interconnected global economy. This event marks a potential end to the era of cheap, reliable Middle Eastern energy, forcing a "regionalization" of supply chains. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has not only spiked prices but has also fundamentally challenged the hub-and-spoke model of international aviation that relies heavily on Gulf-based transit.

Furthermore, this crisis is accelerating regulatory shifts that were previously moving at a much slower pace. Governments are now viewing Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) as a national security imperative rather than just an environmental goal. The U.S. government has fast-tracked the 45Z Clean Fuel Production Credit, providing subsidies of up to $2.00 per gallon for alternative fuels to reduce reliance on foreign petroleum. This geopolitical shock may ironically provide the "green transition" with the financial and political momentum it has lacked for years.

The ripple effects are also extending to the maritime industry, where the closure of the Suez Canal and the Strait of Hormuz is forcing a return to longer, more expensive routes around the Cape of Good Hope. This shift is expected to exacerbate global inflation, as shipping costs for everything from electronics to grain are projected to rise by 15-20% in the coming months.

The Path Forward: Rerouting and Resilience

In the short term, the travel industry must pivot toward survival strategies. Airlines are likely to implement aggressive fuel surcharges and capacity cuts to maintain liquidity. We may also see a wave of consolidation as smaller, less-capitalized carriers struggle to cope with triple-digit oil prices. For cruise lines, the strategic pivot involves shifting fleets away from the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean toward the Caribbean and Pacific, though this will take months to coordinate and may result in a glut of capacity in "safe" markets.

Long-term, the industry’s focus will likely shift toward self-sufficiency. Investment in Alcohol-to-Jet (ATJ) technology, spearheaded by companies like LanzaJet, Inc., is expected to skyrocket. Analysts now predict that SAF could account for 5-8% of global jet fuel consumption by 2028, a massive leap from previous estimates. This "strategic vulnerability" will also likely lead to more robust fuel-hedging programs among airlines that had previously abandoned the practice.

Market opportunities may emerge in the rail and telecommunications sectors as "high-cost travel" becomes the new reality. If oil remains above $100 per barrel throughout 2026, the industry will have to reinvent the value proposition of global tourism, perhaps moving toward longer-duration, less-frequent trips to offset the prohibitive cost of transport.

Final Assessment: A New Geopolitical Reality

The events of March 2026 serve as a stark reminder that the global travel industry remains at the mercy of geopolitical stability. The sharp decline in stocks like United Airlines (NASDAQ: UAL) and Carnival Corp (NYSE: CCL) is not merely a temporary dip but a reaction to a fundamental shift in the global risk landscape. The era of "frictionless" global travel is being tested by the realities of kinetic warfare and energy blockades.

Moving forward, investors should closely monitor the status of the Strait of Hormuz and any potential de-escalation signals from the new Iranian leadership. However, the structural changes—higher defense spending, the acceleration of SAF, and the premium on non-Middle Eastern energy—are likely here to stay. The coming months will determine whether the travel sector can adapt to this "new normal" or if it will face a prolonged period of suppressed growth and heightened volatility.
